Article.ArticleTitle: Automated assessment of regional muscle volume and hypertrophy using MRI.

Abstract.AbstractText: This study aimed to validate a fully automatic method to quantify knee-extensor muscle volume and exercise-induced hypertrophy. By using a magnetic resonance imaging-based fat-water separated two-point Dixon sequence, the agreement between automated and manual segmentation of a specific ~15-cm region (partial volume) of the quadriceps muscle was assessed. We then explored the sensitivity of the automated technique to detect changes in both complete and partial quadriceps volume in response to 8 weeks of resistance training in 26 healthy men and women. There was a very strong correlation (r = 0.98, P < 0.0001) between the manual and automated method for assessing partial quadriceps volume, yet the volume was 9.6% greater with automated compared with manual analysis (P < 0.0001, 95% limits of agreement -93.3 ± 137.8 cm3). Partial muscle volume showed a 6.0 ± 5.0% (manual) and 4.8 ± 8.3% (automated) increase with training (P < 0.0001). Similarly, the complete quadriceps increased 5.1 ± 5.5% with training (P < 0.0001). The intramuscular fat proportion decreased (P < 0.001) from 4.1% to 3.9% after training. In conclusion, the automated method showed excellent correlation with manual segmentation and could detect clinically relevant magnitudes of exercise-induced muscle hypertrophy. This method could have broad application to accurately measure muscle mass in sports or to monitor clinical conditions associated with muscle wasting and fat infiltration.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: P.W., responsible for the automated image analysis in this study, is currently employed by AMRA Medical AB. O.D.L. is co-founder and CSO of AMRA Medical AB. The other authors declare no competing interests.
